questions.scenario:
A storage account has these tags:

```text
service=orders-api
env=prod
owner=platform-api
criticality=customer-facing
```

What do the tags mainly provide?

options.text: Ownership and review context for cost allocation and tuning decisions.
Automatic proof that the storage account is sized correctly.
Automatic proof that all blob data can be restored after any failure.
A replacement for reading the actual storage, retention, and access settings.

questions.explanation: Tags make ownership visible. They do not prove that the resource is right-sized, recoverable, or correctly configured.

questions.scenario: A resource group named `rg-devpolaris-orders-staging` has production-sized compute that has run all month. Tags are missing. What should the team do before deleting it?

options.text: Confirm owner, environment, recent use, and whether it is a rollback or release target.
Delete it immediately because every staging resource is waste.
Assume the name proves it is safe, because resource groups are perfect lifecycle boundaries.
Move it into production without review so the cost feels justified.

questions.explanation: Resource groups are useful boundaries, but they are not enough evidence by themselves. Missing tags and high cost should trigger owner and use review before deletion.

questions.scenario: Which tag keys would help a cost review for `devpolaris-orders-api`?

options.text: `service`, to group resources that belong to the orders workflow.
`env`, to separate production from staging, dev, and test.
`owner`, to route review to a team that can explain the resource.
`favorite-color`, because any tag key gives the same cost allocation meaning.

questions.explanation: Useful tags answer real review questions. Arbitrary tags may be harmless, but they do not improve cost allocation or accountability.

questions.scenario: A shared Log Analytics workspace supports multiple services. The workspace lives in `rg-devpolaris-shared-observability`, and all its cost lands there.

options.text: Decide how shared cost will be reviewed or allocated instead of pretending no service owns it.
Delete the workspace because shared cost cannot be assigned exactly.
Assign the whole cost to the first service alphabetically.
Ignore it because shared resources never create cost leaks.

questions.explanation: Shared resources still need ownership and review rules. The allocation may be approximate, but the cost should not disappear from engineering accountability.
